Title: 'Dire' erosion threat in Penang, NGO says stop-gap fixes not enough
Article:
GEORGE TOWN:  Penang's coastline is undergoing a severe and accelerating erosion crisis, with beaches shrinking, trees toppling and entire stretches of shoreline disappearing during high tide.
Sahabat Alam Malaysia (SAM) president Meenakshi Raman said the situation in key coastal stretches, such as Batu Ferringhi, Teluk Bahang and Tanjung Bungah, was at "very dire" levels, with visible and ongoing loss of beach width compared with a decade ago.
"Trees are being uprooted, beaches are narrowing and in some places they are almost invisible when the tide is high. This is extremely serious," she told the
New Straits Times
.
The worsening condition has triggered mounting concern among hotel operators and residents, particularly in Batu Ferringhi and Tanjung Bungah, where coastal properties now sit closer to the encroaching sea.
Meenakshi said while some short-term mitigation measures, such as granite rock revetments and sandbag placements, were being deployed, these were largely stopgap interventions.
"They are desperate, temporary fixes. They do not address the root of the problem."
Meenakshi warned that the erosion also posed growing risks to public safety and Penang's tourism economy, which is  dependent on its coastal image.
She said the perception of "disappearing beaches" can undermine Penang's long-standing branding as a beach tourism destination.
In Batu Maung, Pantai Esen has reportedly suffered severe degradation, with fisherfolk and local operators describing the area as "ecologically dead" following the appearance of thick, dark sediment.
"This is a tragic loss.
"It raises serious questions when an area that was previously healthy deteriorates so rapidly," she said.
Meenakshi urged authorities not to dismiss concerns linking coastal degradation to large-scale reclamation activities, including ongoing and past projects around the island.
She said the cumulative impact of reclamation works — including developments along Gurney Drive, the southern coastline and the Straits Quay area — must be properly assessed.
[[nid:1431120]]
"When huge volumes of sand and materials are introduced into marine environments, there will inevitably be changes to wave patterns, sediment flow and erosion dynamics.
"This needs to be studied as a whole, not in isolation," she said.
She criticised what she described as fragmented environmental impact assessments, arguing that they failed to capture the broader, cumulative effects of coastal developments.
Meenakshi urged the state government to release reports by the Irrigation and Drainage Department,  the key technical agency overseeing coastal management.
"Good governance demands transparency. The public deserves to know what the technical findings are," she said.
She added that climate change was compounding the crisis, with rising sea levels, stronger wave action and more extreme rainfall increasing coastal vulnerability.
Meenakshi, however, warned that climate factors alone could not be used to dismiss human-driven impacts.
Among immediate solutions, the non-governmental organisation  is calling for a shift towards ecosystem-based coastal protection, including large-scale mangrove restoration to act as natural buffers against wave energy and erosion.
Protection and rehabilitation of seagrass beds and coral reefs, particularly in areas such as Teluk Bahang and Pulau Jerejak, were also highlighted as critical measures.
Meenakshi urged the gazetting of Middle Bank as a marine protected area, citing its importance as a seagrass ecosystem.
SAM is also pushing for a state-wide social and environmental impact assessment covering the island and mainland, as well as a full review of the state's integrated coastal management plan.
"What is needed is a comprehensive climate adaptation strategy that addresses coastal vulnerability in a holistic way," Meenakshi said.
"Without that, we will continue relying on short-term fixes while the coastline continues to erode."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: King Charles grasped 'opportunity' on US trip: Palace
Article:
LONDON: King Charles III's US state visit was a "risk and challenge" that he "grasped in both hands", a palace aide said Sunday as the monarch returned from a widely praised trip.
Charles left for the UK from Bermuda Saturday following what a senior royal aide called a "historic" visit to the United States, aimed at healing strained ties between Washington and London.
The highlight of the four-day visit was the 77-year-old king's speech to US Congress on Tuesday – the first such address since his mother, queen Elizabeth II spoke there after the Gulf War in 1991.
The speech, marking 250 years since American independence, was "high stakes", the aide involved in the visit told reporters.
The king urged the importance of Nato and called for a "just and lasting peace" in Ukraine, despite US President Donald Trump's critical stance on the bloc and Ukraine's leader Volodymyr Zelenskyy.
This was "a measure of how much he personally cares" and proves that he "will always be guided by the truth", the royal aide said.
- 'WARMTH AND LAUGHTER' -
His words won praise from Trump's opponents, with senior Democrat Gregory Meeks hailing the king's "impassioned call for a renewed commitment to Nato."
And the speech appeared to have gone down well with Trump, too.
The president told journalists Friday that Charles "is just a great person. He's a high quality person. I love the job he did in Congress."
The rapport between the king and president continued as they met behind closed doors in the Oval Office, the royal aide said with "an awful lot of warmth and laughter" amid serious topics.
By contrast, Trump has repeatedly lambasted Prime Minister Keir Starmer, particularly over his stance on Iran.
The palace aide denied any awkwardness over the differences in warmth of relationship, stressed the king's visit was on the government's request and there is "not a competition" between the monarchy and Downing Street.
On the contrary, the king and queen "have been able to help the government", the aide said, and the king himself viewed the US leg of his trip as particularly "positive."
"What looked like risk and challenge was also a phenomenal opportunity. One that was grasped in both hands by the King," the aide said.
- 'A PERSONAL TRIUMPH' -
British media lavished praise on the visit: The Daily Telegraph called it "a triumph" and a "reassertion of our shared values", while for The Times, it was a "star-spangled success."
"I think it was a personal triumph," for Charles, royal commentator Richard Fitzwilliams told AFP, citing the king's "superb" speech to Congress, with "brilliant jokes."
"He got it right and every nuance seemed perfect. It pleased everybody," Fitzwilliams said of the address.
The visit proved a "boost" at a "very difficult time for the monarchy," the royal expert said.
Undergoing cancer treatment and after a bitter rift with his son, Prince Harry, Charles this year saw his brother, the former prince Andrew, arrested over ties to the late US sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.
Trump reacted to the royal visit by lifting whisky tariffs, a major trade concession, in honour of Charles.
But Fitzwilliams urged caution on future consequences for the UK, citing Trump's "unpredictability."
Trump "loves all things British royal. But not necessarily the British government," he said.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Penang coastline faces rapid erosion [WATCH]
Article:
audreymd@nst.com.my
GEORGE TOWN: Penang is facing a mounting coastal crisis that could see parts of its shoreline permanently erased within the next 10 to 20 years if current erosion trends continue unchecked.
What was once a gradual and seasonal natural process has evolved into a relentless and accelerating loss of land, with beaches disappearing, infrastructure increasingly exposed and the threat of coastal flooding intensifying, particularly during storm surges.
Malaysia has lost more than 42,500ha of mangrove forests since 2017 due to coastal erosion and human-driven land conversion, stripping away critical natural defences and leaving coastlines more vulnerable to the advancing sea.
The implications are stark. Low-lying coastal areas are becoming harder to defend, raising the real prospect of relocation.
The economic fallout is expected to be severe, particularly for tourism, fisheries and coastal property.
The warning comes from marine expert Professor Datuk Dr Aileen Tan Shau Hwai, who said from a scientific standpoint, coastal erosion in Penang was no longer a distant or seasonal issue.
"It is ongoing and intensifying," she told the
New Straits Times
.
Tan said two decades ago, Penang's shorelines retained a degree of natural resilience, with monsoon-driven erosion often followed by recovery. That balance has since broken down.
"What we are witnessing is a persistent and widespread retreat of the shoreline, with far less opportunity for recovery. In several areas, erosion is occurring year-round.
"This is no longer just an environmental issue — it is affecting infrastructure, tourism assets and coastal communities. In simple terms, the coastline is no longer holding its ground."
[[nid:1431113]]
She said long-identified hot-spots, such as Batu Ferringhi and Tanjung Bungah, have deteriorated significantly over the past decade.
"In many stretches of Tanjung Bungah, the natural beach buffer has disappeared, leaving roads and properties exposed to wave action. The erosion here is continuous, with very limited natural recovery," she added.
Tan said Batu Ferringhi, while still experiencing some seasonal recovery, was showing signs of following a similar trajectory without sustained intervention.
Other vulnerable areas include Sungai Batu, Balik Pulau and parts of Butterworth, where diminishing natural defences are steadily increasing exposure.
The acceleration of erosion reflects a convergence of natural forces and human activity.
Tan said rising sea levels, stronger wave energy and shifting monsoon patterns were intensifying coastal stress, while coastal development, land reclamation and mangrove clearance had weakened the shoreline's natural capacity to absorb these forces.
"Hard engineering structures such as seawalls and groynes may provide localised relief, but they often disrupt sediment movement and transfer erosion further along the coast," she said.
Reclamation projects, she added, must be approached with caution due to their wider and often unintended impact.
Critical ecosystems — including mangroves, sandy beaches and intertidal zones — are being degraded or lost, undermining biodiversity, fisheries and water quality.
At the same time, the economic stakes are rising.
"Coastal tourism depends heavily on beach quality, while infrastructure and coastal properties face increasing exposure to wave action and flooding," she said.
Authorities are beginning to shift from reactive responses to structured intervention.
A RM61 million coastal protection project under the 12th Malaysia Plan will target Batu Ferringhi and Tanjung Bungah from this year, while interim measures such as sandbag barriers have been deployed in critical stretches.
Tan, however, said short-term fixes were insufficient.
"The future lies in a hybrid approach — combining engineered solutions with nature-based strategies, such as mangrove restoration and oyster reefs," she said.
Erosion rates in parts of Penang, she added, were reaching between 0.5m and over 1m annually — a pace that translates into significant cumulative land loss over time.
"The coastline is dynamic, but our response must be proactive, science-based and forward-looking. The longer we delay, the more costly the consequences will be.
"Penang still has time to act — but that window is narrowing," she warned.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump says US could restart Iran strikes 'if they misbehave'
Article:
WEST PALM BEACH, Florida/DUBAI: US President Donald Trump said on Saturday he had been told about the concept of a deal with Iran, but was waiting for the exact wording, while warning there was still the possibility of restarting strikes on the country if Teheran misbehaves.
A senior Iranian official said on Saturday that an Iranian proposal so far rejected by Trump would open shipping in the Strait of Hormuz and end the US blockade of Iran while leaving talks on Iran's nuclear programme for later.
When asked about Iran's proposal before boarding a flight to Miami at West Palm Beach, Florida, Trump replied: "They told me about the concept of the deal. They're going to give me the exact wording now."
He added on his social media channel that he could not imagine the proposals would be acceptable and that Iran had not paid a big enough price for what it had done.
Asked if he might restart strikes on Iran, Trump replied: "I don't want to say that. I mean, I can't tell that to a reporter. If they misbehave, if they do something bad, right now we'll see. But it's a possibility that could happen."
Iran says it is ready for diplomacy
Trump has said repeatedly that Iran can never have a nuclear weapon and said on Friday he was not satisfied with the latest Iranian proposal, while Iran's foreign minister said Teheran was ready for diplomacy if the US changes its approach.
Reuters and other news organisations reported over the past week that Teheran was proposing to reopen the strait before nuclear issues were resolved. The official confirmed that this new timeline had now been spelled out in a formal proposal conveyed to the United States through mediators.
[[nid:1431101]]
Trump also said on Friday that "on a human basis", he did not prefer the military course of action and told congressional leaders he did not need their permission to extend the war beyond a deadline set by law for that day because the ceasefire had "terminated" hostilities.
While saying repeatedly he is in no hurry, Trump is under domestic pressure to break Iran's hold on the strait, which has choked off 20 per cent of the world's oil and gas supplies and pushed up US gasoline prices. Trump's Republican Party faces the risk of a voter backlash over higher prices when the country votes in midterm congressional elections in November.
Iranian media said Teheran's 14-point proposal included the withdrawal of US forces from areas surrounding Iran, lifting the blockade, releasing Iran's frozen assets, payment of compensation, lifting sanctions and ending the war on all fronts, including Lebanon, as well as a new control mechanism for the strait.
The United States and Israel suspended their bombing campaign against Iran four weeks ago, but appear no closer to a deal to end a war that has caused the biggest disruption ever to global energy supplies, roiled global markets and raised worries about the possibility of a wider global economic downturn.
Iran has been blocking nearly all shipping from the Gulf apart from its own for more than two months. Last month, the US imposed its own blockade of ships from Iranian ports.
Washington has repeatedly said it will not end the war, which has led to the deaths of thousands of people, without a deal that prevents Iran from obtaining a nuclear weapon, the primary aim Trump cited when he launched strikes in February in the midst of nuclear talks. Iran says its nuclear programme is peaceful.
Speaking on condition of anonymity to discuss confidential diplomacy, the senior Iranian official said Teheran believed its latest proposal to shelve nuclear talks for a later stage was a significant shift aimed at facilitating an agreement.
Under the proposal, the war would end with a guarantee that Israel and the United States would not attack again. Iran would open the strait, and the United States would lift its blockade.
Future talks would then be held on curbs to Iran's nuclear programme in return for the lifting of sanctions, with Iran demanding Washington recognise its right to enrich uranium for peaceful purposes, even if it agrees to suspend its nuclear programme.
"Under this framework, negotiations over the more complicated nuclear issue have been moved to the final stage to create a more conducive atmosphere," the official said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cops to meet MAHB after KLIA2 deaths
Article:
SEPANG: Police will hold discussions with Malaysia Airports Holdings Bhd (MAHB) to review and strengthen safety measures at Kuala Lumpur International Airport Terminal 2 (KLIA2) following two fatal falls that occurred hours apart on Friday.
KLIA police chief Assistant Commissioner M. Ravi said the engagement aimed to improve safety at the airport and prevent similar incidents.
"Police will hold discussions with MAHB to enhance security measures at KLIA to address this incident soon," he said.
The move comes after the death of a 30-year-old   Algerian    who fell from the third floor of the terminal's  balcony  late Friday, just hours after a woman from China fell to her death near the same location.
Ravi said the latest incident occurred at 9.10pm.
"The man was seen removing his backpack near a safety barrier before falling," he said.
The victim died in Cyberjaya Hospital  at 2.55am.
The   Chinese national died on the spot after falling    around 2.20pm. She was reportedly seen at the Green Zone, Level 3, sitting at the   balcony area.
She had been crying and behaving aggressively.
Security personnel attempted to calm her, but she became agitated and resisted assistance.
"In an effort to prevent further harm, a security officer approached her, but she struggled and subsequently fell into the loading bay area," he said.
Police are calling   those with information on either incident to contact the KLIA police headquarters at 03-8776 8222.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Morocco king names son to senior defence post
Article:
RABAT: King Mohammed VI on Morocco on Saturday appointed his son, Crown Prince Moulay El Hassan, to a senior coordinating post in the north African kingdom's military.
According to a statement from the royal court, the king – himself chief of the military general staff – had "graciously decided to appoint" the 22-year-old "coordinator of the offices and services of the general staff of the Royal Armed Forces."
The king, now 62, had himself held the key position at the heart of the top brass from 1985, after being appointed to it by his father, Hassan II.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Zahid: Standoff in Negri almost over
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:  Umno president Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi believes the political standoff in Negri Sembilan is "almost over", despite the state Barisan Nasional doubling down on its stand to  withdraw support for Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Aminuddin Harun.
"It is almost over. I want to stress here that the issue has nothing to do with
tebuk atap
(backdoor attempt to topple the state government) as claimed by certain quarters," said Zahid, who is  BN  chairman.
"The main issue arose when the
adat
institutions and  state constitution were involved," he said in an exclusive interview during  Umno's 80th   anniversary convention here.
Zahid said the matter would be addressed at a  BN-Pakatan Harapan (PH) political bureau meeting next week.
"I would  like to stress here that the  14 BN assemblymen withdrew support for the menteri besar, not the unity government.
"Many people are mistaken on this point. So  all BN assemblymen will remain in the unity government," he added.
Zahid said he had engaged with state leaders and consulted customary law experts, including Tan Sri Dr Rais Yatim, to better understand the sensitivities surrounding the Adat Perpatih system and the state constitution.
"The core issue arose when the institutions of
adat
and  state constitution were affected, which have been misinterpreted.
"Even those in Negri Sembilan feel that the state constitution and the Undang system must be respected.
"I have discussed this with customary law figures, including Tan Sri Dr Rais Yatim.
"I am clear on the matter and have advised Umno not to interfere if there are polemics regarding these traditions," he said.
On whether Umno is seeking a change in leadership, he said the matter rested with PH.
"We will look at it in a meeting, which is  what we call a consultative council involving our political bureau and their political bureau.
"We leave it to PH to decide. They know what the real issues are and they know the root causes of those problems."
However, pressure continues to mount at the state level.
Earlier, Negri Sembilan BN chief Datuk Seri Jalaluddin Alias said a special assembly sitting should be convened to resolve the crisis through a  vote of no-confidence against Aminuddin.
"This crisis cannot be allowed to continue as it will jeopardise the administration of the government."
"We have sent a letter requesting that this special session be held immediately," said Jalaluddin, who is   Pertang assemblyman.
He said BN had  requested state  Speaker Datuk M.K. Ibrahim Abd Rahman to convene the sitting, adding that BN  had lost confidence in the menteri besar.
Previously, all 14 BN assemblymen withdrew their support for Aminuddin, citing a dispute involving the
Undang Yang Empat and Yang di-Pertuan Besar of Negri Sembilan Tuanku Muhriz Tuanku Munawir.
Jalaluddin said  the state BN would not reverse its decision to withdraw support for Aminuddin.
He  said Umno had potential candidates ready should a change in leadership be required.
He clarified that statutory declarations from the opposition Perikatan Nasional assemblymen were not central to the issue.
"What was submitted by PN merely expressed their willingness or offer of support to stand with Umno and BN," he said.
The situation escalated after BN and Umno assemblymen withdrew support for Aminuddin, prompting speculation of a leadership change, which has since been ruled out at federal level.
Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim  said Aminuddin would continue as menteri besar following a decree by the Yang di-Pertuan Besar of Negri Sembilan.
In the 2023 state election, BN won 14 seats in the 36-seat assembly, while PH secured 17 and Perikatan Nasional five.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kostyuk's lucky shorts steal the show after Madrid triumph
Article:
MADRID: Marta Kostyuk's maiden WTA 1000 title in Madrid came thanks to her power, poise and a pair of unexpected "Lucky Shorts."
The world number 23 beat eighth-ranked Mirra Andreeva 6-3 7-5 in under 90 minutes on Saturday to secure the most prestigious trophy of her career, her third professional singles title and second in less than a month after Rouen.
Yet as the 23-year-old Ukrainian posed for photographs at the Caja Magica, it was not just the silverware that caught the eye.
Held alongside her team and her two dogs, Kostyuk showed off a piece of black men's underwear, prompting raised eyebrows and plenty of laughter.
"We have a tournament laundry service and sometimes the clothes get mixed up and last year we ended up with this piece of underwear where in it was written 'Lucky Shorts'. So we kept it," Kostyuk told Spanish broadcaster Movistar Plus.
[[nid:1431080]]
"My coach Sandra (Zaniewska) kept it as a lucky charm.
"She drove to her father's house just to pick it up before traveling here again for the tournament. And we made this promise that if I'd win we would bring the 'Lucky Shorts' for the photoshoot with the trophy."
Kostyuk was delighted to finally conquer a tournament she had long admired.
"This trophy tastes like champagne, no doubt about it," she said.
"Honestly, watching this tournament as a child and remembering all the people who've won it before me, I never thought this was possible, it's amazing.
"It's one of the toughest tournaments of the year, and everyone plays so well, especially now. So it certainly tastes good, but I just want to enjoy this today and that's it."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Angry Birds'-style video caricaturing Hizbollah draws rebuke
Article:
BEIRUT: A video published by a Lebanese TV outlet caricaturing Hizbollah's leaders and fighters as characters from the "Angry Birds" mobile phone game drew a rebuke from the group, which called the clip "offensive" on Saturday.
On social media, Hizbollah supporters condemned what they considered the ridiculing of leader Naim Qassem, who is also a Shia cleric, with some reacting by sharing images insulting Maronite Patriarch Beshara Rai, the highest Christian authority in Lebanon.
The video, shared by the LBCI channel on Friday, depicts Qassem addressing his fighters — with all of them depicted as birds from the popular video game — as they fight the Israeli army, portrayed as the series' green pigs.
The channel said it deleted the video later on Saturday, after being summoned by Lebanon's judiciary.
Hizbollah said, in a statement, that the video contained "offensive and cheap insults that degrade political discourse to a repulsive level."
The group also called on supporters not to be "drawn into" the controversy "orchestrated by the enemies of the resistance."
LBCI was founded in the 1980s by the Lebanese Forces, a Christian party opposed to Hizbollah. However, the channel distanced itself from the party years ago and has been trying to present a more independent image.
"Before our holy symbols and our sheikh (Qassem), all holy symbols fall," one Hizbollah supporter wrote on X, referring to Maronite Patriarch Rai.
After the wave of insults, Rai was contacted by several officials and religious leaders criticising the rhetoric.
President Joseph Aoun in a statement on Saturday "condemned and rejected any attacks on the heads of Christian and Muslim religious communities and spiritual figures in Lebanon."
He also urged the public "to refrain from personal insults, given the negative repercussions of such practices, especially in the current circumstances the country is going through, which require broad national solidarity."
Lebanese Parliament Speaker and Hizbollah ally Nabih Berri condemned "the campaigns of insult and attacks against religious and national symbols, regardless of their source or the means used, whether in the media or online."
Prime Minister Nawaf Salam, meanwhile, told citizens "to exercise the utmost awareness and reject hate speech to prevent dragging the country into an atmosphere of disastrous strife."
Despite the relative freedom of expression enjoyed in Lebanon compared to other Arab countries, the media, artists and comedians have faced harassment over work deemed by some to be offensive to political or religious figures.
Hizbollah drew Lebanon into the Middle East conflict on March 2 when it fired rockets towards Israel in support of its backer Iran.
More than 2,600 people have since been killed in Israeli attacks, according to Lebanese authorities, with the violence ongoing despite a fragile truce in place since April 17.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Zahid: BN likely to go solo in GE16
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Barisan Nasional is leaning towards going solo in the next general election, although the coalition's top leader has made it clear that flexibility remains on the table.
BN chairman and Umno president Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi said the coalition was "almost certain" to contest independently, while still acknowledging the practical need for political understanding with other blocs.
"We have gone solo before," he said in an exclusive interview for Umno's 80th anniversary celebration.
"We contested solo in GE15 (15th general election) and in the general election before that.
"In the coming election, I am almost certain we will go solo.
"But we should not dismiss the need for an understanding with other party coalitions."
Zahid, who is also deputy prime minister, was responding to questions on whether BN and Umno had finalised their approach for the 16th general election,  particularly on whether they would continue working with Pakatan Harapan or opt for an independent path.
BN and PH have worked together in state and by-elections following the formation of the unity government in 2022.
In GE15, some multicornered fights between BN, PH and Perikatan Nasional split votes in PN's favour.
"We must avoid seat overlaps and three-cornered fights. That is the best approach," said Zahid.
"We will cross the bridge when we come to it."
While the direction appears to be towards contesting solo, he said BN would not be fielding candidates in all 222 parliamentary seats, signalling a more measured and selective strategy.
"I don't think there is a need to do so. We will contest in seats where we believe our chances of winning are high."
Zahid cautioned against treating a solo strategy as a fixed or emotional decision, saying that political miscalculation could leave the coalition exposed.
"We must be crystal clear on this matter. We should not rush into going solo to the extent that we dismiss all possibilities, only to end up 'lost at sea' once again."
He said BN must remain responsive to an increasingly fluid political environment, where alliances could shift and new partnerships could emerge depending on electoral realities.
"Political parties are dynamic and politics is even more dynamic. Many possibilities can arise, especially in choosing political partners who are more compatible with us.
"Because of this situation, Umno must adapt to the prevailing political situation," he said, when asked about possible cooperation with other coalitions, including PN, which recently indicated openness to collaboration.
Despite that openness, Zahid was firm that any cooperation would be bound by non-negotiable principles.
He said BN would not compromise on its core foundations — Islam, the Malay rulers, the special position of the Malays and the status of Bahasa Melayu — which were upheld even during the formation of the unity government with PH.
"During the formation of the government, these four principles were included as part of the 18-point agreement. These four key matters cannot be compromised.
"This is not about a Malay or Islamic agenda alone, but a national agenda as enshrined in our Constitution. And it has been accepted by parties outside BN."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US airlines step up as Spirit winds down
Article:
WASHINGTON: US air carriers on Saturday mobilised to help passengers and crew members stranded by the overnight shutdown of Spirit Airlines, after the low-cost carrier's last-minute talks with creditors and the White House collapsed.
Spirit, known for its bright yellow planes, succumbed to crushing fuel prices and announced in the early hours that it was "winding down its global operations, effective immediately," with all flights cancelled and customer service no longer available.
Other carriers — including American Airlines, Delta Air Lines, United Airlines and JetBlue — moved quickly to scoop up Spirit's customers, offering what some dubbed "rescue fares" to those waking up with cancelled itineraries.
Some airlines said they would increase the number of flights or schedule larger planes in and out of airports where Spirit had a significant presence.
Carriers also sought to support marooned Spirit staff — and hire them.
Spirit has been in and out of bankruptcy since 2024, and the White House was recently considering a bailout.
"The recent material increase in oil prices and other pressures on the business have significantly impacted Spirit's financial outlook," the company said in a statement.
"With no additional funding available to the company, Spirit had no choice but to begin this wind-down."
It has promised refunds.
The company had nearly 7,500 employees at the end of last year, according to filings. Unions representing them slammed the failure to reach a deal.
"The pain of this decision will not be felt in boardrooms. It will be felt by pilots, flight attendants, mechanics, dispatchers, and ground crews, and by the families and communities that depend on them," said the Air Line Pilots Association.
Like a dog on a bone
US Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy scrambled to defend the position of President Donald Trump's administration as a rescue plan never materialised.
"The president was like a dog on a bone trying to figure out a way to keep Spirit afloat," Duffy told a press conference Saturday at Newark Liberty International Airport in New Jersey.
"In the end, this was a creditor issue. Again, they have the final say of whether they want to do a deal with the government," he added.
"But also from the government's perspective, we oftentimes don't have a half a billion dollars laying around in a spare account that we can put into a bailout of an airline."
Duffy blamed the administration of Trump's predecessor Joe Biden for blocking a proposed merger between Spirit and JetBlue in March 2024 — and assured ticket holders that they would get refunds.
[[nid:1431064]]
Changing plans
Florida resident Ramon, 60, said he had planned to visit family in Honduras this week.
He and his son Kevin saw headlines in recent days about Spirit's woes and contacted the airline, but opted not to take a refund offer, as alternative flights were more expensive and there were no immediate signs that the airline would fold.
"I was trying to go today on another airline, but it was like $1,000 a ticket," Ramon told AFP, asking only to be identified by his first name.
The family now says they will wait for the refund and book a flight in early June.
Fuel costs
Launched in 1992, Spirit Airlines became one of the first low-cost carriers in the United States.
Between February 2025 and January 2026, it carried some 28 million passengers, according to government data.
At the same time, it has been limping along since announcing bankruptcy in November 2024 and again in August 2025.
In late February, Spirit said it had reached an "agreement in principle" to restructure its debts and that it expected to emerge from bankruptcy by early summer.
Days later, the United States and Israel began bombing Iran, leading to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, which sent jet fuel prices soaring.
Hopes of a White House bailout began to fade last week as oil prices spiked and creditors were reportedly furious with the stake the government planned to take in the company.
Bradley Akubuiro, a crisis management expert at Bully Pulpit International, said while fuel prices may have been the final nail in the coffin, Spirit was "already in a very difficult position."
"The more lasting consequence is that one of the strongest sources of low-fare pressure in the US market is gone," Akubuiro told AFP.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Senior Iranian officer says fresh conflict with US 'likely'
Article:
TEHERAN: A senior Iranian military officer said on Saturday that renewed fighting with the US was "likely", hours after President Donald Trump said he was not satisfied with an Iranian negotiating proposal.
Iran delivered the new draft to mediator Pakistan on Thursday evening, state media reported, without detailing its contents.
The war, launched by the United States and Israel in late February, has been on hold since April 8, with one failed round of peace talks having taken place in Pakistan.
"At this moment I'm not satisfied with what they're offering," Trump told reporters, blaming the stalled talks on "tremendous discord" within Iran's leadership.
He added that the decision he faced was between whether to "just blast the hell out of them" or to "try and make a deal", saying he would rather not take the first option.
On Saturday, Mohammad Jafar Asadi, a senior figure in the Iranian military's central command, said "a renewed conflict between Iran and the United States is likely."
"Evidence has shown that the United States is not committed to any promises or agreements," he added, in quotes published by Iran's Fars News Agency.
Deputy foreign minister Kazem Gharibabadi told diplomats in Teheran "the ball is in the United States' court to choose the path of diplomacy or the continuation of a confrontational approach."
Iran, he said, was "prepared for both paths."
Hypocritical
The White House has declined to provide details on the latest Iranian proposal, but news site Axios reported that US envoy Steve Witkoff had submitted amendments to a previous one putting Teheran's nuclear programme back on the negotiating table.
Iran's mission to the United Nations pointed to the massive US nuclear arsenal, accusing Washington on Saturday of "hypocritical behaviour" towards Iran's own atomic ambitions.
There was no legal "restriction on the level of uranium enrichment, so long as it is conducted under the International Atomic Energy Agency's supervision, as was the case with Iran", it said, using the abbreviation for the UN nuclear watchdog.
News of the new Iranian proposal had briefly pushed oil prices down nearly five per cent, though they remain about 50 per cent above pre-war levels amid the ongoing closure of the Strait of Hormuz.
Iran has maintained a stranglehold on the strait since the war began, choking off major flows of oil, gas and fertiliser to the world economy, while the United States has imposed a counter-blockade on Iranian ports.
[[nid:1431105]]
The vice speaker of Iran's parliament, Ali Nikzad, said that under draft legislation being considered for managing the waterway, 30 per cent of tolls collected would go towards military infrastructure, with the rest earmarked for "economic development."
"Managing the Strait of Hormuz is more important than acquiring nuclear weapons," he said.
Fighting meanwhile continued Saturday in Lebanon, where Israel has carried out deadly strikes despite a separate truce with the Iran-backed armed group Hizbollah.
The Israeli military said it had struck dozens of Hizbollah targets across southern Lebanon following evacuation warnings for nine villages. Hizbollah, for its part, claimed several attacks targeting Israeli troops.
The Israeli strikes included one in the village of Yaroun on what its military called a "religious building", which was damaged.
The French Catholic charity L'Oeuvre d'Orient said the troops had "destroyed" a convent belonging to the Salvatorian Sisters, a Greek-Catholic religious order with which the charity is affiliated.
Terminated
In Washington, D.C., lawmakers were wrestling over whether Trump had breached a deadline to seek congressional approval for the war.
Administration officials argue the ceasefire paused a 60-day clock, after which congressional authorisation would be required — a claim disputed by opposition Democrats.
"There has been no exchange of fire between United States forces and Iran since Apr 7, 2026," Trump said in letters to congressional leaders, adding that the hostilities "have terminated."
In Iran, the war's economic toll is deepening, with oil exports crimped and inflation surging past 50 per cent.
"Everyone is trying to endure it, but... they are falling apart," 40-year-old Amir, a Teheran resident, told an AFP reporter based outside the country.
"We still have not seen much of the economic effects because everyone had a bit of savings. They had some gold and dollars for a rainy day. When they run out, things will change."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Israel strikes several parts of south Lebanon, says Hizbollah
Article:
BEIRUT: Israel carried out strikes across southern Lebanon on Saturday, its military saying it hit dozens of Hizbollah targets.
The attacks have become a regular occurrence, despite a fragile ceasefire in the Israel-Hizbollah war that has seen both sides accuse each other of violations.
In a statement, the Israeli military said it had struck Hizbollah targets, adding that "approximately 70 military structures and approximately 50 Hizbollah infrastructure sites were dismantled across several areas" on Saturday.
Lebanon's state-run National News Agency (NNA) also reported a series of Israeli strikes across southern Lebanon with several killed, including three in the town of Sammaiyeh in the Tyre district.
The Israeli army had earlier issued two evacuation warnings for nine southern villages.
Hizbollah claimed several attacks targeting Israeli troops, saying they were in response to ceasefire "violations".
[[nid:1431051]]
Israel has kept up deadly strikes on Lebanon despite the Apr 17 ceasefire that sought to halt more than six weeks of war between it and Hizbollah.
The ceasefire text grants Israel the right to act against "planned, imminent or ongoing attacks".
Lebanese army chief Rodolphe Haykal on Saturday met with visiting US General Joseph Clearfield, the head of a five-member committee in charge of monitoring a 2024 ceasefire meant to put an end to the last war between Israel and Hizbollah.
They discussed "the security situation in Lebanon, regional developments, and ways to maximise the effectiveness of the (committee) and enhance its operations", said a Lebanese army statement.
Israeli strikes have killed more than 2,600 people in Lebanon and displaced more than a million since Hizbollah drew the country into the Middle East war on March 2 in support of its backer Iran.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Americans on a budget mourn loss of low-cost Spirit Airlines
Article:
NEW YORK: US travelers lamented news of the shutdown of Spirit Airlines on Saturday, saying that the closure of the ultra-low-cost carrier eliminates one of the few air travel options for low-income and working-class Americans. Spirit abruptly cancelled flights overnight, stranding passengers and staff around the US, Caribbean and Latin America, after collapsing under financial pressures that included a sharp rise in fuel costs due to the Iran war.
On social media platforms such as Reddit and X, where disgruntled passengers often vent their frustrations about delayed or cancelled flights, former Spirit patrons recounted how the airline had provided a lifeline to money-conscious travelers.
"They truly were one of the last cheap – 'get me there as fast and cheap as possible' – options," Reddit user AioliUpset7805 wrote on a thread about the airline's closure. "I'll miss them."
Spirit, which had operated commercially since the early 1990s, became known for providing some of the cheapest available flights in exchange for limited luxuries and services. Unlike most U.S. airlines, Florida-based Spirit charged for bottled water, for example, and did not provide reclining seats.
While that lack of amenities sometimes became the topic of online memes, many travelers said Spirit cleared the way for Americans on a budget to take family holidays or to visit loved ones living in far-flung parts of the country.
"I can only imagine how many millions of families (there are) out there where vacations are now out of reach," Reddit user BigBubby305 said, adding that the price difference between Spirit and carriers like Delta and American Airlines was, at times, more than US$1,000 for a set of tickets for their family.
OTHER AIRLINES SEEK TO FILL GAP
At the Orlando International Airport overnight, a digital departure display sign was filled with bright red notifications of cancelled Spirit flights that had destinations everywhere from Nashville to San Juan, Puerto Rico.
Following Spirit's halting of operations, multiple US carriers – including Frontier, JetBlue and Southwest – introduced discount fares and plans for new summer routes.  Airlines like Delta and American Airlines were also offering temporarily lower fares to Spirit passengers.
Spirit's closure comes at a time of rising prices across the American economy, which have been made worse by the US-Israeli war against Iran.
The demise of Spirit, which according to data from aviation analytics firm Cirium had more than 4,000 domestic flights scheduled through May 15, and a recent doubling of jet fuel prices, are expected to heap more costs on American travelers. "I always took great pride in knowing we were saving people money and allowing those to travel who couldn't afford to otherwise," wrote Reddit user Coryntrevors, who said they piloted Spirit's signature bright yellow Airbuses out of Las Vegas for over a decade.
"To shut down forever tonight has been one of the saddest experiences of my life."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 17 foreigners held for failing to produce valid travel documents
Article:
KUCHING: Seventeen foreigners were detained in an operation around the city yesterday.
Sarawak Immigration Department said in a statement today that 16 of those held were Indonesians, while one was a Pakistani national.
All were unable to produce valid passports or travel documents during inspections carried out at several premises, including workers' quarters and rented rooms.
The operation, which involved 28 enforcement officers, also included thorough checks at locations identified as foreign nationals' settlements.
"The operation aims to detect and take action against foreign nationals who violate immigration laws," the statement said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Umno must acknowledge past mistakes to regain public support, says KJ
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Restoring Umno's image will be an uphill task for the party to recover public support, but the first step is for the party to acknowledge its past mistakes, Khairy Jamaluddin said.
The former Umno Youth chief said he agreed with Umno vice-president Datuk Seri Johari Abdul Ghani's assessment that
the party's image
had not fully recovered.
"To some extent, yes. I think that we still have an uphill task in trying to recover public support. Acknowledging the mistakes is just the first step," said Khairy on the sidelines of the Umno Convention held in conjunction with the party's 80th anniversary.
"After that sets the stage for us to perhaps reintroduce ourselves to swing voters or the middle ground who perhaps have lost trust with us.
"Then it's to what the president said. It's not enough for us just to reminisce about the past, but also come up with a new set of ideas, a new set of suggestions of what we would give to the voters if we are given the opportunity to govern again," he added.
On whether Umno had enough time to turn things around before the 16th General Election, Khairy pointed to the surge in online membership registrations as a positive sign, but stopped short of declaring the battle won.
"There's still more than one and a half years and I think we have momentum with us.
"But between the hope that's being offered by some of our leaders, Johari's reminder is also extremely timely — that it's not fully come back yet."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Myanmar man found dead after fall into lake
Article:
KAJANG: A Myanmar man was found dead after he was believed to have fallen from a lakeside embankment in Taman Cheras Prima here today.
The victim, identified as Kyaw Zaw Hein, was found at a depth of about 1.5 metres, about three metres from the lake's edge.
Selangor Fire and Rescue Department operations assistant director Ahmad Mukhlis Mukhtar said they received an emergency call at 7.31pm before a team from the Seri Kembangan Fire and Rescue Station was dispatched to the scene.
Ahmad Mukhlis said the Water Rescue Unit, which was near the location, immediately carried out a search operation using the arch pattern method.
"The victim was found shortly afterwards before being brought up to the bank by rescue personnel.
"The body was later handed over to police for further action," he said in a statement today.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UAE exit may not tank oil prices
Article:
THE United Arab Emirates' withdrawal from Opec is widely seen as lessening the clout of the producer group and initiating a race to boost output, ultimately resulting in sharply lower crude oil prices.
However, the United States and Israeli war against Iran has upended global crude markets to such an extent that expecting what might otherwise seem the most obvious outcome is likely flawed thinking.
It may be the case that the UAE decision to leave the Organisation of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (Opec) does substantially weaken the group, and there may ultimately be more crude supplied by the UAE once, or perhaps if, pre-war shipment volume resumes through the Strait of Hormuz.
But these two outcomes are not the only possible consequences, nor are they as guaranteed as they may seem.
The first question is just how damaged Opec is by the move.
Losing the fourth-largest producer in the group is a blow.
An important fact is that the UAE  and Opec's de facto leader Saudi Arabia are the two exporters most able to ramp up production rapidly.
But the 65-year-old producer group has weathered departures before and still managed to remain relevant, and exercise influence over the supply — and therefore the price — of global crude oil.
Angola left in 2024, Qatar in 2019, Ecuador for the second time also in 2020, Indonesia in 2016 and Gabon in 1995, although Gabon later rejoined.
It could be argued that none of these countries were as significant as the UAE, although Angola and Qatar would still count as major losses.
It would be a brave energy analyst to back a view that Saudi Arabia and Opec+ member Russia are weakened substantially because of the loss of a member that produced about 12 per cent of Opec's output.
Much will depend on how Saudi Arabia and Russia respond and whether they decide to enter a volume and price war.
Such a move would aim to push high-cost producers  out of the market, with the primary target being US shale output.
While US President Donald Trump has welcomed the UAE decision to leave Opec, the irony is that if the move does result in a volume and price war, it is US energy companies that will bear the brunt.
Falling retail fuel prices may be great politically for Trump and his Republican party, but if this is the outcome it does underscore that the mercurial US leader is not necessarily as big a friend of his own country's energy industry as he claims to be.
But the main question mark over the UAE move is whether this will indeed result in a volume and price war.
The UAE was shipping about 3.3 million barrels per day (bpd) prior to the Feb 28 US and Israeli attacks on Iran and the resulting  closure of the Strait of Hormuz.
Most analysts estimate that the UAE could increase fairly rapidly to 4.5 million bpd and hit five million bpd in the medium term.
This of course assumes that the Strait of Hormuz is fully and sustainably reopened at some point.
But even assuming the strait returns to pre-war flows, at least for crude oil, will the extra barrels that the UAE may supply be enough to spark a significant price correction?
Much depends not only on what other exporters do, but also on what tactics importers adopt.
Do importers try to rebuild depleted inventories as rapidly as possible, fearful of another  conflict, or do they take a measured approach in the expectation of moderating prices?
China, the world's biggest crude importer, has traditionally built stockpiles up during periods of low prices and cut imports when prices rise to levels its refiners view as too high.
Will China even bother restocking given it has not used much of its massive inventories, estimated to be at least 1.2 billion barrels.
US crude exports are likely to fall back once the Strait of Hormuz is fully open, but how rapidly it will rebuild inventories is less certain. There are also questions as to how quickly production in the Middle East can return to pre-war levels, given that fields have been shut and facilities damaged by drones and missiles.
Simply put, the Iran conflict has created too many variables, meaning that any conclusions on how the UAE withdrawal from Opec will affect the group and the wider supply-demand balance carry an unusually high degree of uncertainty.
* The writer is from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sabah records 357 monkey malaria cases, one death
Article:
SANDAKAN: Sabah has recorded 357 monkey malaria cases and one death in the first four months of this year, with Ranau, Kudat and Tawau identified as the main hotspots, Health Minister Datuk Seri Dr Dzulkefly Ahmad said.
"This is quite serious. Early detection and immediate treatment are key. All health clinics and hospitals are capable of detecting these monkey malaria cases," he said after launching the Wellness Hub here today.
Dzulkefly said close cooperation with the Wildlife Department and the Forestry Department was crucial in tackling the life-threatening disease.
Monkey malaria, also known as zoonotic or simian malaria, is transmitted from monkeys to humans through mosquito bites and is typically caused by the parasite Plasmodium knowlesi.
Meanwhile, Dzulkefly said the Wellness Hub initiative was an important catalyst in strengthening the country's healthcare ecosystem towards a more comprehensive and proactive approach.
He said the programme encouraged communities to adopt healthier lifestyles by shifting the focus from treatment to prevention.
"This approach is increasingly urgent, as the government's annual cost of managing non-communicable diseases (NCDs) has reached RM64.3 billion, exceeding the Health Ministry's total budget of RM46.52 billion," he said.
He added that the burden of NCDs in Sabah remained a concern, with high rates of obesity (50.4 per cent), high cholesterol (32.1 per cent), smoking prevalence (24.9 per cent) and sedentary behaviour (57.7 per cent).
"There are 38 Wellness Hubs nationwide, including five in Sabah — Sandakan, Lahad Datu, Kudat, Keningau and Putatan. My ambition is to have at least one Wellness Hub in every parliamentary constituency. We want to take a 'prevent before sickness' approach," he said.
Dzulkefly said the Wellness Hub also served as a strategic platform to address stunting and malnutrition among children.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: NST Leader: RM710mil PACE offers relief, but no shield from global recession risks
Article:
THE RM710 million Progressive Acceleration for Capability and Employment (PACE) economic "shock absorber" could not come at a more seismic economic juncture.
PACE is the response to the impasse at the Strait of Hormuz energy chokepoint. It is a pragmatic and prudent policy to strengthen labour market resilience. It is not a "shield" or a recession-proof wall, but a first line of defence to soften impending economic earthquakes.
In retaliation to the United States-Israel bombings, Iran littered the Hormuz with sea mines, deployed gunboat attacks and missiles against departing oil tankers linked to Iran's foes.
Immediately, transport of 20 per cent of the world's oil and gas supplies froze, except shipments for Iranian allies or those willing to pay a toll for safe passage.
Nevertheless, the Hormuz squeeze triggered severe global shortages of crucial commodities, with repercussions felt by consumers worldwide.
The conflict has spiked petrol prices, kicking up inflation and recession risks. Touted as the biggest oil supply disruption in history, it bumped up shipping costs, frayed insurance and threatened food security through fertiliser shortages.
On the onset, this must be clearly stated: PACE is designed to cushion shocks to the Malaysian labour market, but it won't shield workers from the expected recession.
PACE, announced this week by Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim, focuses on job enhancements, protection, retraining and wage maintenance. These are economic "shock absorbers", but they are not a "force field".
PACE is designed to address possible retrenchments in the manufacturing, logistics, retail and export sectors, a softener for income losses. It should aid workers' move into less-affected sectors and help employers to reduce retrenchments.
Still, PACE's reach is limited: RM710 million sounds big, but it covers only 500,000 workers. The biggest provision of RM580 million goes to the Employment Insurance System and social safety net for workers who lose jobs.
PACE provides unemployment insurance, human resources training, job placement, gig worker skills programmes and TalentCorp industrial training grants. PACE's broader mission is critical: if recession — oil shocks, inflation and export slowdown — hits, Malaysia could be confronted with economic losses in the billions.
PACE is unable to stifle inflation caused by the Iran conflict because it affects oil and fuel, shipping and freight, fertilisers, petrochemicals, plastics and, more critically, food prices.
It is beyond PACE to stop household cost-of-living pressures because if a global recession slashes demands from the US, China and the European Union, Malaysia's exports of electronics, gloves, machinery and commodities will weaken, regardless of domestic labour programmes.
Realistically, PACE should mitigate mild-to-moderate disruptions of oil spikes, slower growth and retrenchments. Bigger fiscal measures might be needed later in the form of higher fuel subsidies, cash transfers, wage subsidies and broader small and medium enterprise support.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Elderly couple killed in Teluk Intan crash
Article:
IPOH: An elderly couple were killed after the car they were travelling in was involved in a two-vehicle collision at Batu 5, Jalan Maharajalela near Teluk Intan this afternoon, leaving three people pinned in the wreckage.
Perak Fire and Rescue Department (JBPM) Operations Division assistant director Sabarodzi Nor Ahmad said in the 2.40pm incident, the local couple, both aged 70, died at the scene after being trapped inside the Toyota Vios they were travelling in.
He said the crash involved a Proton Persona and a Toyota Vios, with all three victims pinned in the vehicles when rescuers arrived.
"Firefighters used special hydraulic rescue tools and a stretcher to extricate all the victims from the wreckage," he said in a statement today.
Sabarodzi said another victim, a 63-year-old local man who was driving the Proton Persona, sustained injuries to his hands and legs after being trapped in the driver's seat.
The injured victim was handed over to Health Ministry personnel for further treatment, while the two deceased victims were handed over to the police for further action.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MACC records 22 statements in RM1.1 billion semiconductor investment probe
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) has recorded 22 statements in its investigation into alleged abuse of power and misappropriation involving a RM1.1 billion investment deal involving the Economy Ministry.
MACC senior investigations director Datuk Seri Mohd Hafaz Nazar said the statements included those from ministers and senior officers from several government agencies since the investigation into the case started on Feb 13 last year.
He also confirmed that the MACC will call Datuk Seri Rafizi Ramli on Monday to assist in the investigation.
He said the economy minister is expected to have his statement recorded at the Putrajaya MACC headquarters about 10am on May 4.
The probe centres on a strategic collaboration agreement between the Economy Ministry and chip design firm Arm Holdings involving the semiconductor industry.
Hafaz added that political analyst James Chai, whose real name is Chai Jin Shern, had his statement recorded over three consecutive days starting last Tuesday.
He said the case is being investigated under Section 23 of the MACC Act 2009.
Yesterday, Rafizi said he had been summoned by the MACC for questioning over the government's joint venture with the chip design firm.
He said the investigation centres on allegations that he had pressured the cabinet to expedite approval of the deal, which critics claim was rushed and detrimental to the country.
Rafizi also denied any wrongdoing, stressing that there was no financial motive or monetary gain involved.
On April 30, Chai was seen arriving at the MACC headquarters for a third day to assist in the investigation.
The case is being investigated under Section 23 of the MACC Act 2009 following complaints by several non-governmental organisations, which alleged that the agreement was made hastily and could have financial implications for the government.
The investigation is also examining possible elements of conflict of interest, including claims that certain individuals were appointed to senior positions in the foreign company after leaving a ministry.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Missing girl often played at site where she was swept away, says mother
Article:
KAJANG: A young girl feared drowned after falling into a drain and being swept away by strong currents in Bandar Mahkota Cheras yesterday often played at the location where the incident occurred.
The victim's mother, Nordina Zulkarnain, 34, said the area, which includes a field and playground, is a popular spot for children living in the apartment, including her daughter, Nur Qaseh Deandra Mohd Qayyum.
"Yesterday, she was playing as it was a holiday, together with other children who live in the apartment.
"After the incident, her elder brother, who is my second child, went up to the house and informed me and my husband," she told reporters when met here today.
Nordina said her husband then contacted emergency services to seek assistance from the Fire and Rescue Department.
Search and rescue (SAR) operations today also involved the victim's family members, including her father, grandfather and uncle, who assisted along the riverbanks.
Commenting on items found near the search area, including slippers and a ball, Nordina confirmed they did not belong to her daughter.
Meanwhile, the victim's grandfather, Zulkarnain Ahmad, 57, who rushed to the scene from Seremban yesterday, expressed deep concern and distress over the incident.
"She is the third of four siblings. I am very close to her. I have not been able to sleep since yesterday.
"Today, I joined the rescue team in searching for her in the surrounding area," he said.
Yesterday, media reported that a nine-year-old girl was feared drowned after falling into a drain while attempting to retrieve a ball, before being swept away by strong currents.
The Fire and Rescue Department received an emergency call on the incident at Kondominium Sri Hijau at 7.08pm.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Meth user arrested over Toyota Vios theft in Pontian
Article:
PONTIAN: A 44-year-old man was arrested within 24 hours in connection with the theft of a black Toyota Vios and tested positive for methamphetamine.
Police said he has multiple criminal and drug-related offences.
District police chief Superintendent Hadzrat Hussein Mion Hussain said police received a report of a missing car on 2 May at 1.00am.
The vehicle was reported parked by the roadside at Telok Kerang, Kukup.
He said following the report, personnel from the Criminal Investigation Division and Task Force Unit of the Pontian District Police Headquarters, assisted by personnel from Permas Police Station, carried out an operation in Parit Panjang and Parit Mohayat, Ayer Baloi, Pontian.
The operation was conducted in Parit Panjang and Parit Mohayat, Ayer Baloi, Pontian.
"Through the operation, police intercepted the vehicle and arrested a 44-year-old local suspect.
"A check on the suspect's records found several previous offences, including two violent and property crime cases and five drug-related offences.
"A urine test also returned positive for methamphetamine," he said in a statement today.
Hadzrat Hussein said the suspect has been remanded for four days from 2 to 5 May, and the case is being investigated under Section 379A of the Penal Code and Section 15(1)(a) of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952.
He said initial investigations found negligence on the part of the complainant, the vehicle owner, who did not switch off the engine and left the key inside the car.
"Criminals are always looking for opportunities to carry out minor or major criminal activities.
"Therefore, members of the public are advised to remain vigilant and cautious in such cases in order to help prevent crime from occurring," he said according to Harian Metro.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: KJ praises Zahid for acknowledging 'shortcomings on both sides'
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Datuk Seri Ahmad Zahid Hamidi's speech calling for Umno to move past grudges seems to strike a chord with Khairy Jamaluddin, who was recently readmitted into the party.
The former Umno Youth chief said he was moved by the party president's address at the Umno Convention, which reflected his sincerity in welcoming back former leaders and members under the Rumah Bangsa initiative.
"It reflects his sincerity in reopening the doors of Umno for me.
"It is extremely rare that you will see a leader of the party acknowledge that there were shortcomings on both sides.
"I'm thankful for his magnanimity and for his sincerity," Khairy said on the sidelines of the convention held in conjunction with Umno's 80th anniversary today.
Earlier, Zahid in his address called on p
arty members to put aside past grievances and internal divisions
as the party sets its sights on its next phase of growth.
He said the party's eight-decade journey had been defined not only by achievements, but by its resilience in overcoming repeated crises.
Khairy said Zahid's words amounted to a clean slate, highlighting that the president had used the occasion to deliver what he described as a pointed piece of fatherly advice.
"He stressed that loyalty is important. He said it's not important to be loyal to anyone in the party, but just to be loyal to the party.
"I think that was a very sharp piece of advice — a fatherly way of reminding me about my next period in Umno," Khairy said.
Khairy also said he agreed with Umno vice-president Datuk Seri Johari Abdul Ghani's assessment that
Umno's image
had not fully recovered.
"To some extent, yes. I think that we still have an uphill task in trying to recover public support," he said.
Additionally, Khairy cautioned against treating the acknowledgement of past mistakes as sufficient in itself.
"Acknowledging the mistakes is just the first step. After that sets the stage for us to perhaps reintroduce ourselves to swing voters or the middle ground who perhaps have lost trust with us.
"Then it's to what the president said. It's not enough for us just to reminisce about the past, but also come up with a new set of ideas, a new set of suggestions of what we would give to the voters if we are given the opportunity to govern again," he said.
On whether Umno had enough time to turn things around before the 16th General Election, Khairy pointed to the surge in online membership registrations as a positive sign, but stopped short of declaring the battle won.
"There's still more than one and a half years and I think we have momentum with us.
"But between the hope that's being offered by some of our leaders, Datuk Seri Johari's reminder is also extremely timely — that it's not fully come back yet."
He said the priority for the coming months was clear.
"What we do in the next one and a half years or before the general election is extremely important: to focus on acknowledging the mistakes, and to start thinking about what it is that we can offer the public and the voters in the run-up to the general election," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Today's news in pictures - Saturday, May 2, 2026
Article:
MISSED what happened today? Here's a glimpse at what happened throughout the country in photographs.
TUMPAT – Grape farmer Wan Yusoff Abdul Rahman ventures into agri-tourism farming at his orchard in Kampung Belukar near Wakaf Bharu. NSTP/WANNA ABDUL RAHIM
